1: Ps 116: Individual Thanksgiving After Deliverance from Death — Setting for the Cup of Salvation. Ps 116 is an individual thanksgiving psalm with an unusually personal and intimate register. The psalmist addresses YHWH directly throughout and narrates his near-death experience and rescue in the first person. The opening is striking: 'I love the LORD' (ahavti ki yishma YHWH et qoli, I love because YHWH heard my voice). The ahav (love) is the relational term of covenant: the same verb used for YHWH's love for Israel (Deuteronomy 7:8) now comes from the human side. The ground of love is experiential: YHWH heard my voice — the particular, personal act of hearing.
2: 'Because he inclined his ear to me, therefore I will call on him as long as I live' — 'inclined his ear' (hittah ozno li) — the image of YHWH bending down to listen: the sovereign stooping to hear the petitioner. This posture of divine attention became the ground of the psalmist's lifelong prayer habit: 'therefore I will call on him as long as I live' (uveyamai eqra). The personal experience of answered prayer creates the pattern for all future prayer.
3: 'The snares of death encompassed me; the pangs of Sheol laid hold on me; I suffered distress and anguish' — 'snares of death' (apaphuni chevlei mavet) — the cords or nets of death surrounding the psalmist. 'Pangs of Sheol' (umetzarei she'ol metza'uni) — the anguish of Sheol's distress found him. The language of near-death experience: the psalmist was within Sheol's grasp. The 'pangs' (metzarim, straits/distress) may refer to a severe illness or life-threatening circumstance whose specifics the psalm does not disclose.
4: 'Then I called on the name of the LORD: O LORD, I pray, deliver my soul!' — the rescue cry: 'called on the name of the LORD' (uveshem YHWH eqra) — the divine Name as the address for prayer. 'O LORD, I pray, deliver my soul' (ana YHWH malleta naphshi) — the petition's simplicity: deliver my naphesh (soul/life). The ana (please, I pray) is an urgent petition particle, marking the seriousness of the request.
5: 'Gracious is the LORD, and righteous; our God is merciful' — the theological confession prompted by the rescue: 'gracious' (channun), 'righteous' (tzaddiq), 'merciful' (mrachem). These three divine attributes are not abstract doctrines but experientially verified through the psalmist's rescue. The OT theology of divine character is always grounded in historical experience — the attributes are known through the acts.
6: 'The LORD preserves the simple; when I was brought low, he saved me' — 'preserves the simple' (shomer peta'im YHWH) — the peti (simple one) — the naive, inexperienced person who lacks cunning. The divine protection of the simple/naive is unexpected: YHWH protects those who have the least ability to protect themselves, not those with the greatest skill. 'When I was brought low, he saved me' (daloti veli yehoshia) — the poverty-rescue pattern: at the nadir of weakness, YHWH acts.
7: 'Return, O my soul, to your rest; for the LORD has dealt bountifully with you' — 'return to your rest' (shuvi naphshi limenuchayichi) — the psalmist addresses his own soul, calling it back to its covenant resting place. The menuchah (rest) — the covenant rest that was disturbed by the near-death crisis — is restored. 'The LORD has dealt bountifully with you' (ki YHWH gamal alaichi) — gamal (to deal bountifully, to ripen, to wean): the word covers the full process of bringing something to completion and fullness. YHWH has brought the psalmist's experience to a full and good conclusion.
8: 'For you have delivered my soul from death, my eyes from tears, my feet from stumbling' — the three-part rescue: from death (mavet), from tears (dim'ah), from stumbling (dochi). The rescue is comprehensive: not just physical survival but the removal of grief and the restoration of stable walking. The three are a descending scale: from the ultimate danger (death) to the emotional response (tears) to the physical instability (stumbling). YHWH addressed all three levels.
9: 'I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living' — 'walk before the LORD' (ethalech lifnei YHWH) — the covenant walk in YHWH's presence (Genesis 17:1: 'walk before me and be blameless'). 'In the land of the living' (beartzot hachayim) — the phrase contrasts with the realm of the dead (Sheol, the land of silence): the psalmist will live, not die, and his living will be a covenant walk in YHWH's presence.
10: 'I believed, even when I spoke: I am greatly afflicted' — Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 4:13 ('I believed, and so I spoke') as the paradigm of apostolic faith: belief that speaks even under suffering. The original context: the psalmist maintained faith (he'emanti, perfect: I have believed) even while confessing affliction (ani anitiy meod, I am greatly afflicted). Faith does not require silence about suffering; the Psalms model the co-existence of belief and lament.
11: 'I said in my alarm, All men are liars' — 'in my alarm' (vani amarti vechapzi, in my haste/panic) — the emotional state of crisis producing an overgeneralization: 'all men are liars' (kol haadam kozev). The panic-statement is neither endorsed nor condemned by the psalm — it is honestly reported as what was said in extremity. The contrast between the panic-confession (v11) and the covenant-walk resolution (v9) shows the range of human experience within authentic faith.
12: 'What shall I render to the LORD for all his benefits to me?' — the question of reciprocity: mah ashiv laYHWH (what shall I return to YHWH?). 'His benefits' (kol tagmulohi, all his dealings/benefits toward me) — the gamal root again (cf. v7). The psalmist's question is not rhetorical despair but genuine reflection: given everything YHWH has done, what appropriate response is there? The answer follows in the next verses.
13: 'I will lift up the cup of salvation and call on the name of the LORD' — 'the cup of salvation' (kos yeshu'ot essa) — the specific vessel of the todah (thanksgiving sacrifice): the cup of wine raised in the thanksgiving ceremony in the temple courts. The 'cup' imagery has a rich trajectory: the cup of blessing in the Passover seder (the third cup was the 'cup of blessing,' cf. 1 Corinthians 10:16) and ultimately the Eucharist cup of the new covenant. The cup of salvation is raised and the divine name called on — public testimony accompanying the libation.
14: 'I will pay my vows to the LORD in the presence of all his people' — 'pay my vows' (nedarai laYHWH ashalem) — the vow made in distress (v4: 'I pray, deliver my soul') is fulfilled publicly. 'In the presence of all his people' (negdah na lekhol amo) — the public fulfillment in the temple assembly. The ANE vow-system required public fulfillment before the community: the covenant community witnessed the transaction between worshiper and YHWH.
15: 'Precious in the sight of the LORD is the death of his saints' — 'precious' (yaqar, costly, valued, rare) — 'the death of his saints' (hamaveta lahasidav) is precious to YHWH. The verse is the most-quoted in Christian funeral liturgy and is interpreted in two ways: (1) YHWH does not allow his saints to die lightly or needlessly — their deaths are 'costly' to him, treated with great care; (2) YHWH values the death of his faithful as something that comes at high cost and is not easily conceded. Neither interpretation makes death desirable but both affirm divine attentiveness to the deaths of covenant people.
16: 'O LORD, I am your servant; I am your servant, the son of your maidservant. You have loosed my bonds' — 'I am your servant' (ani avdechah) — double identification as eved (servant). 'The son of your maidservant' (ben amatchah) — born into the divine household: the son of a maidservant was a permanent, household servant (unlike a purchased slave who might be freed). 'You have loosed my bonds' (pittachta lemoseirai) — the rescue from the near-death 'bonds' of vv3-4 is now described as liberation. The servant who was bound is now freed — still a servant, but no longer in death's bonds.
17: 'I will offer to you the sacrifice of thanksgiving and call on the name of the LORD' — 'sacrifice of thanksgiving' (zevach todah) — the specific sacrificial category (Leviticus 7:12-15): a peace-offering accompanied by leavened and unleavened bread, eaten on the day of offering, requiring the whole household and invited guests. The todah (thanksgiving/confession) sacrifice was the OT ceremony of public gratitude after deliverance. The todah connection to the Eucharist is deeply rooted: Jesus's Last Supper in the Passover context used Hallel psalms that include todah language.
18: 'I will pay my vows to the LORD in the presence of all his people' — the refrain from v14 repeated, creating a structural bracket. The double statement of public vow-fulfillment (vv14,18) emphasizes the communal, non-private nature of covenant gratitude. Individual rescue produces communal testimony. The vow paid in public before 'all his people' is the psalm's answer to the question of v12.
19: '...in the courts of the house of the LORD, in your midst, O Jerusalem. Praise the LORD!' — 'the courts of the house of the LORD' (bechatzerot beit YHWH) — the specific location: the temple courts where the todah sacrifice was offered and where the vow-fulfillment would be witnessed. 'In your midst, O Jerusalem' (betocheki Yerushalaim) — the city is addressed directly; the testimony of rescue takes place in the capital of the covenant community. The 'Hallelujah' closes the psalm, sending the testimony outward from the individual thanksgiving to universal praise.
